A tough security week for the U.S.
economy.
The stock market made a steep decline then staged partial rebound.
Federal Reserve board made a surprising cut in a key lending rate.
And the White House and Democratic leaders made a rare and quick agreement on an economic stimulus plan.
But as NPR's Brian Naylor reports, there's still another hurdle before any checks are in the mail.
The ink is barely dry on the agreement House leaders hammered out this week with the Bush administration, and already there's talk of how it might be changed in the Senate.
The $150 billion stimulus plan relies on a combination of individual tax rebates and business tax cuts to spur the economy.
The president, who spoke to Republican House members at their annual party retreat in West Virginia yesterday, says he thinks it's fine the way it is.
President GEORGE W.
BUSH: I understand the desire to add provisions from both the right and the left.
I strongly believe it would be a mistake to delay or derail this bill.
(Soundbite of applause)
The agreement is likely to sail unscathed through the House within the next 10 days according to the speaker, Democrat Nancy Pelosi.
